books.title: You Can Feel It Before They Say It
books.subtitle: For the manager who carries the team: when your body sounds the alarm for a crisis that never comes
books.desc: You read the room before you read your own chest — bracing for a crisis that, most days, never comes. This is a book about the false alarm that fires in the body of every manager who carries the people, and about what it costs to stay on call with no one on call for you.
books.long_desc:
You read the room before you read your own chest.

By the time you notice your heart is going, the meeting is already over. You're in the hallway, or the parking garage, or finally home, and your body is still braced — scanning the team, the numbers, the one person who went quiet on the call, the email you haven't answered. Nothing is on fire. Everyone is, technically, fine. And still some part of you is standing guard, certain that if you stop holding it all, something will fall.

This book is for the manager who carries the people. The one who absorbs the room's stress so the team doesn't have to, who can feel a direct report's bad week before they say a word, who has quietly decided that caring this much is just the cost of doing the job well. You are good at this. That's the problem. The same nervous system that makes you a steady place for everyone else has started sounding an alarm that never fully shuts off — not because you're failing, but because it has been on call for too long with no one on call for you.

What you have is not a character flaw, and it is not weakness dressed up as empathy. It is a false alarm: a finely tuned threat-detection system firing in a room where there is no real danger, only the ordinary, endless weight of being responsible for other people. Your body learned to treat every flicker of someone else's distress as a fire to put out. It has forgotten how to tell the difference between a crisis and a Tuesday.

Chapter by chapter, this book does three things, and none of them is a fix. First, recognition — it names the alarm precisely enough that you can feel it start, the tightening in the chest, the reach for the phone, the reflex to manage one more feeling that isn't yours. Then descent — it lets you go down into the cost you've been outrunning, the emptiness under the competence, the cup you keep pouring from and never refill, instead of bracing past it one more time. And finally integration — not a cure, not a morning routine, but a different relationship with a body that has been trying, all along, to get your attention.

The practice this asks for is small enough to happen where your life actually happens: on a delayed flight with the laptop open, in the ninety seconds between back-to-back calls, in the car before you walk in the door and become available to everyone again. You won't be asked to care less. You'll be asked to notice when the alarm is firing for a danger that isn't there — and to let that be information instead of an order.

You will not finish this book repaired. The role will still ask too much. The people will still need you, and you will still, being who you are, want to show up for them. But you may stop mistaking your nervous system's exhaustion for proof that you have to keep bracing. You may learn what your body has been saying under all that noise. And you may find that you can keep caring without disappearing into it.

books.title: Everyone on the Unit Seems Fine
books.subtitle: For the nurse who thinks she's the only one barely holding on — easing the ache of measuring your anxious insides against everyone who looks calm
books.desc: You look around the unit and everyone else seems steady, unbothered, fine — and you feel like the only one quietly coming apart. This is a body-based companion for the nurse who measures her anxious insides against everyone else's calm outsides and always comes up short. It won't help you win the comparison. That's the point.
books.long_desc:
You scan the station and they all look fine. The charge nurse is unflappable. The one who started the same year you did seems to have it handled. Everyone moves like the work isn't quietly taking pieces of them, and you stand there with your heart going and your stomach in a knot, certain that you are the only one who isn't built for this — that everyone else got some steadiness you missed, and that if they could see inside you, they'd know.

This book is for the nurse who measures her insides against everyone else's outsides. The one who compares her anxiety, which she feels in full, to their composure, which is all she can see — and always loses. You are not weaker than the people around you. You're just doing the one thing that guarantees you'll come up short: holding your own raw, unedited interior up against the calm, curated surface everyone else is showing, including you. They can't see your bracing. You can't see theirs. And so each of you walks the unit privately convinced you're the only one barely holding on.

Here you'll come to recognize comparison as a body state, not a fact — the sink in the chest when someone else looks fine, the heat of not-enough, the way your nervous system reads their calm as evidence against you. You'll learn that you have been pointing a precise, high-stakes attention — the same care that makes you a good nurse — at exactly the wrong target: yourself, measured against a standard nobody actually meets. And gently, you'll practice coming back into your own body, your own ground, where the only nervous system you can actually tend is the one that's yours.

This will not help you win. There's no protocol here to finally come out ahead of the people around you, because that race was never real and never had a finish line. It asks the opposite: that you set the measuring down, just for a breath, and let yourself be exactly as okay or not-okay as you are — without grading it against a room full of people doing the same hiding you are.

Nothing here will help you measure up. That's the whole point. What you've never been given is permission to stop measuring at all. That is what this book is for.

You are allowed to be the most anxious one in the room and still belong in it. You always did.

books.title: Off the Clock, Still on Alert
books.subtitle: For the nurse whose body never got the page that the shift is over — meeting the alarm that keeps firing when there's no longer anyone to save
books.desc: You spend your shift braced for the alarm that means someone is dying — and then you go home and your body keeps bracing anyway. This is a gentle, body-based companion for the nurse whose nervous system learned to stay ready and forgot how to stand down. This isn't one more thing to push through. It's a way to finally let your shoulders come down.
books.long_desc:
You are in the grocery store, in the shower, in bed beside someone you love — and your chest is tight, your jaw is set, your whole body is leaning toward a door that isn't going to open. There's no monitor here. No call light. Nothing is wrong. And still some part of you is waiting for the sound that means run.

This book is for the nurse whose alarm system never learned to go quiet. The one who can stay calm in an actual emergency and then come apart in a parking lot over nothing. You are not broken, and you are not weak. Your body did exactly what the work asked of it: it learned to stay ready, all the time, for years. The bracing kept you sharp on the unit. The problem is that nobody ever taught it how to come back down — and so it doesn't.

Here you'll come to recognize the false alarm for what it is. Not danger. Not a sign you're losing it. Just a nervous system firing on an old memory of how often the real thing came. You'll feel where you hold it — the floor of the belly, the set of the jaw, the breath you take high in the chest and never quite finish. And you'll learn, slowly and on your own timeline, how to signal to your body that the shift is over. That it's allowed to come off alert. That the warmth is allowed to come back into the places that braced.

This will not make you faster or tougher. It will not optimize you for the next twelve hours. There is no protocol here to master and no metric to hit. If anything, it asks you to do the one thing the job never lets you do: stop, and let your body catch up to the fact that, right now, in this moment, you are safe.

Nothing here will help you push through. That has never been your problem. You push through for a living. What you've never been given is permission to come down — and that is what this book is for.

You are allowed to set the alarm down. Even if you don't yet believe it's safe. Especially then.

Book 1 of the Felt Sense Publishing anxiety series.

books.title: The Ones You Couldn't Save
books.subtitle: For the nurse whose anxiety is grief that was never given room — making space for the losses you carried home and never set down
books.desc: Some of your anxiety isn't fear of what's coming. It's grief for what already happened — the patients you lost, the goodbyes there was no time to feel, the losses you carried out of the room and never set down. This is a tender, body-based companion for the nurse whose body is holding mourning it was never given room to do. It won't rush you past it. It will help you finally let it move.
books.long_desc:
You called the time, and then you charted, and then you went to the next room, because that is what the shift required. There was no pause. No space to feel the weight of what just happened. And so the loss went somewhere — it didn't disappear — it went into your body, and it has been sitting there, under the anxiety, for longer than you've let yourself notice. The tightness in your chest some mornings isn't only fear of the day ahead. Some of it is everyone you couldn't bring back, still waiting to be grieved.

This book is for the nurse carrying losses she never had time to mourn. The one who has held strangers' families through the worst moment of their lives and never once been given the room to fall apart over her own accumulating goodbyes. You are not unfeeling, and you are not weak for still carrying them. The job demanded that you keep moving, and you did — but a body cannot be asked to witness loss after loss without somewhere to put it, and yours has been holding all of it, alone, in the dark.

Here you'll come to recognize how much of your anxiety is grief that was never given room — the ache that lives beneath the bracing, the throat that tightens, the tears that arrive at strange times for no reason you can name. You'll learn, gently and slowly, that grief isn't something to push through or get over; it's something to let move through a body that's been clenched against it. You'll make space for the goodbyes you never got to feel, and you'll discover that what you've been calling anxiety has, underneath it, been a kind of love with nowhere to go.

This will not give you closure on a list. There is no protocol here to file your losses away, because they were never paperwork. It asks something tenderer: that you stop bracing against the grief and let it soften, let it surface, let it finally be felt — so it no longer has to keep knocking from inside your chest.

Nothing here will help you move on faster. The moving-on-fast is what trapped it. What you've never been given is permission to stop, and grieve, and let the warmth come back. That is what this book is for.

You are allowed to mourn them now. All of them. The grief was only ever the size of how much you cared.

books.title: Too Many Things at Once
books.subtitle: For the nurse carrying six patients, a dozen alarms, and no room left to breathe — finding ground when the whole shift arrives at the same second
books.desc: Six patients, three alarms going off at once, a phone that won't stop, and a body that has run out of room to hold any of it. This is a body-based companion for the nurse who is drowning in the all-at-once — too much arriving with no space left to think. It won't help you do more. It will help you find the floor under your feet when the flood comes.
books.long_desc:
Two call lights, a pump alarming, a doctor on the phone, a family in the hallway, and the med you were three steps from pulling still not pulled. It all arrives in the same instant and there is no order to it, no space between, no second where you can hear yourself think. Your breath goes shallow and high. Your mind goes white. The to-do list stops being a list and becomes a wave, and you are somewhere underneath it.

This book is for the nurse who lives in the flood. The one whose capacity gets exceeded by ten in the morning and who spends the rest of the shift just trying to keep her head above it. You are not disorganized and you are not falling behind. You are a human nervous system being asked to hold more inputs than any nervous system was built to hold, over and over, with no time to discharge what came before the next thing lands.

Here you'll come to recognize overwhelm in the body before it takes you under — the breath climbing into the chest, the shoulders rising, the strange narrowing where the room seems to shrink. You'll learn that you don't have to clear the flood to find ground; the ground was never in the task list. It's under your feet, in the weight of your body in your shoes, in one full breath that reaches the floor of the belly. You'll practice the small, real things that bring you back into a body the work keeps pulling you out of — not to make you faster, but to give you somewhere to stand while the wave passes through.

This is not a productivity system. It will not teach you to triage your soul the way you triage a hallway. It asks for the opposite: that in the middle of too much, you take thirty seconds to come back to one thing — the floor, the breath, the warmth returning to your hands — and let that be enough.

Nothing here will help you carry more. You already carry too much, and your body knows the exact weight of it. What you've never been given is a way to set even a corner of it down without dropping it. That is what this book is for.

You are allowed to come back to one breath. The flood will still recede. You don't have to outrun it to survive it.

Book 2 of the Felt Sense Publishing anxiety series.

books.author: Dora Devlin

books.title: The Nurse Who Should Be Able to Handle It
books.subtitle: For the nurse hiding the anxiety behind competence — meeting the quiet belief that needing help means something is wrong with you
books.desc: You're the one who holds it together. The capable one, the steady one — and underneath it, a belief that if anyone saw how anxious you really are, they'd know something is wrong with you. This is a tender, body-based companion for the nurse who has been overworking to cover a defect that was never there. It won't ask you to perform okay. It will help you set the mask down.
books.long_desc:
You are good at this. People trust you. You're the nurse other nurses come to, the one who stays calm when it counts, who picks up the extra shift, who never lets it show. And somewhere underneath all of it lives a quiet, corrosive certainty: that the anxiety you carry is proof of a flaw — that a real nurse, a good one, wouldn't feel this way — and that if anyone ever saw the whole of it, they would finally understand that something is wrong with you.

This book is for the nurse who hides the anxiety behind competence. The one who has made herself indispensable partly so no one will look too closely. You are not defective, and you never were. Somewhere along the way you learned that your worth was your usefulness, that needing care was a liability in someone whose whole job is to give it, and so you buried the part of you that struggles and worked twice as hard to make sure it stayed buried. The overworking isn't ambition. It's a cover. And it's exhausting in a way that has nothing to do with the hours.

Here you'll come to recognize shame as a thing the body holds, not a verdict that's true — the heat that climbs the neck, the urge to disappear, the bracing in the chest that says don't let them see. You'll begin to feel the difference between the anxiety itself and the shame about having it, and you'll learn that the second one, the hiding, is most of the weight. Gently, on your own timeline, you'll practice letting warmth back into the places you've kept armored, and meeting the struggling part of you the way you'd meet a frightened patient — not with a fix, but with the simple radical act of staying.

This will not make you more impressive. It will not help you perform okay more convincingly. It asks the opposite: that you stop covering, just for the length of a breath, and discover that the defect you've been hiding was only ever a nervous system asking to be cared for too.

Nothing here will help you hold it together better. You hold it together for a living, and it's costing you everything. What you've never been given is permission to be the one who isn't okay. That is what this book is for.

You are allowed to need care, too. The capable ones most of all.

Book 4 of the Felt Sense Publishing anxiety series.

books.title: Did I Chart That
books.subtitle: For the nurse replaying the shift at 2am — easing the loop of one worry becoming a thousand when the thought won't let the body sleep
books.desc: It starts with one thought — did I chart that, did I catch that, did I miss something — and by 2am it has become a thousand. This is a gentle, body-based companion for the nurse whose mind replays the shift on a loop that feeds itself. It won't argue you out of the spiral. It will help your body step off the wheel that the thinking can't.
books.long_desc:
It's a single thought at first. Did I document that order. Did the night nurse catch what I flagged. Was that patient's color off, and did I say something, and was it enough. One worry, reasonable enough — and then it forks, and forks again, and an hour later you are lying in the dark having mentally re-run the entire shift and lost every round. Your heart is going. Your stomach is tight. The thoughts are moving too fast to hold and too loud to leave, and the more you try to think your way to the bottom, the deeper the wheel turns.

This book is for the nurse who lies awake litigating a shift that is already over. The one whose mind treats every small uncertainty like a thread that must be pulled until something unravels. You are not careless — the opposite. You care so precisely, and the stakes have been so real for so long, that your mind has learned it can never fully stand down. So at night, when there's finally nothing to do, it does the only thing it knows: it scans, and it spirals.

Here you'll come to recognize the spiral as a body event, not just a thought event — the racing that lives in the chest, the churn in the belly, the way rumination is fed by a nervous system that won't come off watch. You'll learn that you cannot win the argument the spiral is having, because the spiral doesn't want an answer; it wants to keep moving. So instead of chasing the thoughts, you'll practice gently bringing your body back to ground beneath them — the weight of you in the bed, the long slow breath out, the warmth returning to a chest that's been braced — until the loop loses the fuel it runs on.

This will not give you certainty about the shift. There is no protocol here to guarantee you missed nothing, because that certainty was never available to anyone. It asks something kinder: that you stop demanding your mind solve, at 2am, what only the morning can answer, and let your body rest while it waits.

Nothing here will help you think your way out. The thinking is the wheel. What you've never been given is permission to step off it without first reaching the bottom. That is what this book is for.

You are allowed to let the thought be unfinished. The morning is allowed to hold what the night cannot.

books.title: The Part of You That Never Stops Scanning
books.subtitle: For the nurse whose eyes are always on the monitor — softening the hypervigilant mind that keeps watch for dangers that never come
books.desc: Your eyes go to the monitor before you've decided to look. You scan every room, every face, every drip, always one step ahead of the thing that might go wrong. This is a body-based companion for the nurse whose hypervigilant mind never goes off duty — even when she's home, even when she's safe. It won't take your watchfulness away. It will help you let it rest.
books.long_desc:
You walk into a room and your eyes have already swept it — the IV, the color, the rise and fall of a chest, the alarm that's about to sound. You catch the thing before it happens, every time, because catching it is the job. But the scanning doesn't stop when the shift does. You watch the road too hard. You read faces for trouble at the dinner table. Some part of you is always on the perimeter, monitoring for the emergency, and it has been so long since it stood down that you've forgotten you're the one keeping it up.

This book is for the nurse whose watcher never sleeps. The one whose hypervigilance saves lives at work and quietly runs her ragged everywhere else. You are not paranoid and you are not high-strung. You trained a part of your mind to scan for danger constantly because, for years, the danger was constantly real — and that part learned its job too well to simply quit when you clock out. The watching kept your patients safe. It's keeping you, now, from ever fully arriving in your own life.

Here you'll come to recognize the watcher as a body that never came off alert — the eyes that dart, the shoulders that won't drop, the breath held high and shallow against a threat that, right now, isn't there. You'll learn to thank that vigilant part instead of fighting it, because it has been trying to protect you all along. And gently, on its own timeline, you'll begin to show it the difference between the unit and your living room, between on-duty and home, until it learns that it's allowed — sometimes, in safe places — to lower its eyes and let the ground hold you instead.

This will not dull the edge that makes you good at what you do. There's no protocol here to switch off your awareness, and you wouldn't want one. It asks something gentler: that you give the watcher permission to rest when the watching isn't needed — so the same nervous system that keeps everyone safe finally gets to feel safe itself.

Nothing here will help you watch more carefully. You already watch carefully enough for ten people. What you've never been given is permission to look away. That is what this book is for.

You are allowed to put the watch down. The danger is not in this room. You can let your eyes close.

books.title: The Losses No One Sent Flowers For
books.subtitle: For the nurse grieving in a hallway between rooms — the patients, the version of you who started, and the love for this work you're afraid you've lost
books.desc: Nurses grieve constantly and almost never out loud — the patient who didn't make it, the colleague who left, the bright-eyed person who started this career and isn't here anymore. This book is for the grief you've been carrying between rooms with no time to set it down. It won't rush you toward closure. It will simply, finally, let you mourn.
books.long_desc:
There was no funeral for most of it. The patient you sat with at the end of your shift, who was gone by the start of your next one — and you had four others to chart, so you washed your hands and kept moving. The version of you who came into this work wide open, who cried in the supply closet the first time and meant it. The certainty that you were helping. You've lost so much, quietly, and the world keeps handing you a fresh assignment before the last loss has even landed.

This book is for the nurse who is grieving and hasn't been allowed to. Not the tidy, ceremonial grief the world makes room for — the other kind. The cumulative, unwitnessed losses that pile up in a body that never gets to stop and feel them. You have buried more than anyone knows, and you did it standing up, in scrubs, with a smile for the next family.

You'll come to recognize grief where you didn't know to look for it: in the numbness that isn't peace, in the irritability that surprises you, in the strange flatness where your love for this work used to be. You'll feel how unmourned loss settles into the body and waits — the throat that tightens, the weight behind the sternum, the breath you don't quite take. And gently, at your own pace, you'll begin to let some of it move. Not to get over it. To finally honor it.

This is not a stage-by-stage program for getting to acceptance on schedule. Grief is not a task to complete, and you are not behind. Nothing here will ask you to find the silver lining, to be grateful for the lesson, or to push the sorrow down so you can be functional by Monday. You've been functional. That was never the problem.

What it offers instead is a place to set it down. The witness you never got. The slow, embodied permission to mourn what was taken and to release what you've been carrying for people who will never know your name — so that warmth can return to the places grief has held shut.

You are allowed to grieve. All of it. Even now. Even the parts no one sent flowers for.

Book 2 of the Felt Sense Publishing Burnout series.

books.title: There Is No Room Left In Me
books.subtitle: For the nurse carrying more than any one body can hold — when the shift never ends and there's nowhere to put any of it down
books.desc: Some shifts don't end when you clock out — they just keep arriving, alarm after alarm, until there's no room left in you to feel any of it. This book is for the nurse who is full past the brim and still being asked to hold more. It won't add one more thing to your list. It will help you find, somewhere in your own tight chest, a little room to breathe.
books.long_desc:
It arrives all at once. The call light, the page, the family at the desk, the order that should have been signed an hour ago — and underneath all of it, the patient in 4 whose face you will see tonight when you finally close your eyes. You are holding so much that you've stopped being able to feel where one thing ends and the next begins. There is simply no room left in you.

This book is for the nurse who is flooded. Not lazy, not weak, not bad at the job — the opposite. You are good enough at it that everyone keeps handing you more, and you keep taking it, because that's what you do. But capacity is not infinite, and a body that has been at the edge of its banks for months doesn't need to be told to be more resilient. It needs somewhere to overflow that isn't the drive home in the dark.

You'll come to recognize the flood for what it is: not a character flaw but a nervous system that has had too much arrive at once for too long, with no margin to recover between waves. You'll feel how overwhelm lives in the body first — the held breath, the shoulders up by your ears, the clench you forgot wasn't normal. And slowly, without forcing anything, you'll find that there are small thresholds inside you where a little room opens. A breath that goes all the way down. A moment between tasks that belongs to you and not the floor.

This is not a productivity system. It will not teach you to do more in less time, or to optimize your way back to the version of you who could carry it all. There is no carrying it all. That was never the job, even when it felt like it. Nothing here asks you to push through, override what you feel, or perform okayness for one more soul.

What it offers instead is room. Permission to set something down. The felt sense, returning quietly, that you are a person and not a vessel — and that the flood does not get the final word about how much of you is left.

You are allowed to take up space in your own life again. Even now. Even tired. Even full.

Book 1 of the Felt Sense Publishing Burnout series.
books.angle: You ran out of room a long time ago, and no one noticed but you.
books.author: Quinn Reyes

books.title: Your Shoulders Still Carry the Shift
books.subtitle: For the nurse whose body won't come down off the floor — jaw set, hands braced, still holding the weight of twelve hours long after you've hung up your badge
books.desc: You're home, the house is quiet, and your body still won't believe the shift is over — listening for a monitor that isn't there, replaying every chart, braced for a call that never comes. This book is for the nurse whose vigilance never goes off duty. It won't tell you to relax. It will help your body finally believe it's safe to.
books.long_desc:
The house is quiet. Everyone's asleep. And you are lying there fully awake, listening — for an alarm, a call bell, the particular tone that means someone is crashing. There's nothing to listen for. You're home. But the part of you that spent twelve hours scanning for the next emergency never got the message that the shift is over, so it keeps scanning the dark, finding threats in the silence, refusing to stand down.

This book is for the nurse whose mind never clocks out. The hypervigilance that makes you good at your job — the way you catch the subtle change before the numbers do, the way you're three steps ahead of the worst thing that could happen — doesn't switch off when you badge out. It follows you home. It sits at the foot of your bed. It replays the shift looking for the mistake you might have made, and it keeps your body braced for an emergency that already passed, or never came.

You'll come to recognize the watcher for what it is: not anxiety to be ashamed of, but a nervous system that learned, shift after shift, that letting its guard down is dangerous. You'll feel how that bracing lives in the body — the shallow breath, the jaw that won't unclench, the eyes that won't quite close. And slowly, without being forced or talked out of anything, you'll offer that vigilant part of you what it has needed all along: enough safety to set the watch down for a while.

This is not a course in clearing your mind or thinking positive. You can't willpower your way out of a body that's convinced it's still on the floor, and trying only adds one more thing to monitor. Nothing here asks you to override what you feel, push the alertness away, or perform calm you don't have. Calm isn't something you force. It's something a safe-enough body remembers how to do.

What it offers instead is the slow return of the ground beneath you. The felt sense that the danger is not in this room. Permission, at last, for the watcher to rest — so that your own quiet house can finally feel like a place you're allowed to be off duty.

You are allowed to stop scanning. Even now. Even with your eyes open in the dark.

Book 3 of the Felt Sense Publishing Burnout series.

books.title: Your Body Is Still Listening for the Call Light
books.subtitle: For the nurse whose nervous system never clocked out — bracing in the dark for an emergency that ended hours ago
books.desc: The alarm in your chest at 2am isn't a warning — it's an echo. After years of staying ready for the worst, your body learned to keep watch even in a quiet, safe room. This is a gentle, body-first way to let it finally stand down.
books.long_desc:
The room is dark and the room is safe. The shift ended hours ago. And still your chest is tight, your jaw is set, and some part of you is half-listening for a monitor, a call light, the sound of someone needing you fast. Nothing is wrong. So why does your whole body insist that something is?

This book is for the nurse whose alarm system never learned how to turn off. You've spent years being the calm one in the room while your own nervous system ran hot underneath — reading every change, staying a half-step ahead of the worst thing that could happen. That readiness kept your patients safe. Now it lies down beside you at night and won't let go, firing in the quiet, bracing for danger that isn't coming.

You'll come to recognize the false alarm for what it is: not a sign that you're broken, and not a threat to outthink, but an old protector that doesn't yet know the emergency is over. books.title: The Version of Me That Could Do This
books.subtitle: For the woman grieving the self who used to have boundless energy — burnout as a loss, and what the body has to release before it can recover
books.desc: Burnout isn't only exhaustion. It's a loss — the quiet death of the version of you who could keep all the plates spinning, who said yes to everything, who didn't need this much rest. This book is for the woman mourning who she used to be, and learning that recovery begins not with pushing harder, but with letting that self go.
books.long_desc:
No one warned you that burnout would feel like grief. You keep waiting to bounce back, to return to the woman who used to run on four hours of sleep and a coffee, who said yes before the question finished, who could carry it all and make it look effortless. And slowly it's dawning on you that she isn't coming back — and that something in your chest aches when you admit it.

This book is for the woman in mourning for a self she hasn't finished losing. You may not have words for it yet. It shows up as a heaviness behind the breastbone, a tightness in the throat, a tiredness that sleep doesn't touch. You're grieving the energy you used to have, the version of you who thrived on the pace, the future you'd quietly assumed would arrive if you just kept going. And under the grief, often, is the harder question: who am I if I can't do it the way I used to?

You'll come to recognize that what you're carrying isn't only fatigue — it's loss that hasn't been allowed to move. Grief lives in the body, in the held breath and the braced shoulders and the throat that won't quite let the tears come. You'll learn to let it be there without rushing it or fixing it. You'll feel the difference between collapse and release — between giving up and finally setting down something you were never meant to carry forever. And in the space that opens when you stop fighting the loss, you'll begin to sense who you actually are now, underneath who you used to be.

This is not a book about getting your old self back. It won't promise to restore your former capacity or hand you a plan to be that version of you again. That woman did her best, and she earned her rest. What's here instead is a way to honor the loss — to let your body grieve what burnout took, so that something quieter and more sustainable can grow in its place.

You are allowed to mourn her. You are allowed to be tired in a way you've never been. And you are allowed to become someone new, slowly, from the ground up.

books.title: The Day Doesn't Fit Inside Me Anymore
books.subtitle: For the capable woman whose body has reached its edges — when the deadlines, the messages, and the asks all flood in at once and your nervous system has no shore left
books.desc: There's a moment when the inbox, the calendar, the people who need you, and the dishes in the sink all reach you in the same breath — and your chest goes tight, and you can't find the first thread to pull. This is a book for the woman who keeps functioning while quietly drowning, written not to help you do more but to make room in a body that has none left.
books.long_desc:
It usually arrives in the body before it arrives in words. Your chest tightens. Your breath climbs up into your shoulders. Three things are happening at once and then it's seven, and somewhere in there you stop being able to find the first thing to do — so you freeze, or you move faster, and either way the flood keeps rising.

This book is for the woman everyone calls capable. The one who holds it together at work, holds it together at home, answers the message, makes the appointment, remembers the thing nobody else remembered. From the outside you look like someone managing. Inside, you've run out of room. There's too much arriving and nowhere left to put it, and you've started to wonder if the overwhelm is just who you are now.

It isn't. Overwhelm is not a character flaw and it's not a scheduling problem you can solve with a better system. It's a nervous system past its capacity, bracing against a flood it was never meant to hold all at once. So this book doesn't start with your calendar. It starts with your body — with the held breath, the clenched jaw, the shoulders that have been up near your ears for so long you forgot they could come down.

You'll come to recognize the flood for what it is: not a moral test you keep failing, but a wave passing through a system that needs room to let it pass. You'll learn to feel the ground under your feet again when everything above it is too much. You'll find the difference between doing the next thing and bracing against all the things — and you'll feel, maybe for the first time in a long while, a small amount of space open back up inside your own chest.

This is not a productivity book. It will not teach you to fit more in, move faster, or optimize your way to calm. There is no morning routine here that will fix you, because you are not broken. What's here is slower and kinder than that: a way to put some of it down, on purpose, and let your body discover it can.

Nothing in these pages will help you carry more. That's the point. You've been strong enough for long enough. You're allowed to make room.

Book 1 of the Felt Sense Publishing Burnout series.
books.angle: You didn't fail the day. The day arrived all at once, and your body had no more room.
books.author: Quinn Reyes

books.title: The Part of You That Never Clocks Out
books.subtitle: For the woman whose mind keeps scanning long after the workday ends — and how to teach a braced body that it is finally safe to settle
books.desc: Even when the laptop is closed, some part of you stays alert — replaying the meeting, bracing for the email, scanning for the thing you might have missed. This book is for the woman whose vigilance never quite lets go, and who's beginning to understand that the exhaustion isn't coming from the work. It's coming from the watching.
books.long_desc:
The workday ended hours ago. The laptop is shut. And still, some part of you hasn't put it down — running the meeting back, drafting the reply you haven't sent, combing the day for the loose thread that might unravel while you sleep. You lie down and your body stays braced, breath held high in your chest, your attention circling the edges of everything, waiting for a problem that never quite arrives. You are exhausted, and you cannot, for the life of you, come down.

This book is for the woman whose alertness never ends with the day. The one who's always a step ahead of the problem, always anticipating, always smoothing the thing over before it becomes a thing. It made you good at your work. It made you the person everyone leans on. And it is quietly wearing you down to the bone — because a body that never receives the signal that it's safe to rest will keep burning through energy you no longer have to give.

You'll come to recognize the watching for what it is: not a flaw, not a personality trait, but a part of you that learned, somewhere along the way, that staying alert was how you stayed safe. You'll feel where it lives in the body — the shallow breath, the tightness behind the eyes, the shoulders that won't fully drop even when nothing is wrong. You'll learn to notice the scanning without obeying it, and to offer your body the message it has been missing: the day is done, nothing here needs you right now, it's safe to let go. Not by force. By degrees. The way a grey sky loosens and clears after a long, heavy afternoon.

This is not a book about discipline or shutting your mind off. It won't hand you a trick to silence your thoughts or a routine to perfect your evenings. You cannot think your way out of a body that's braced; you can only, gently, show it that it's safe to soften. That work is slower and kinder than any quick fix, and it's the only thing that truly lasts.

The watching kept you safe once. You don't need it the way you once did. You're allowed to let your guard soften, breath by breath, and discover that the ground holds you — that the world stays whole even when you stop holding it together yourself.

Book 3 of the Felt Sense Publishing Burnout series.

books.title: The Alarm That Won't Switch Off at Night
books.subtitle: For the exhausted high-achiever whose body braces for danger the moment the lights go out — and a gentler way to tell it you're safe
books.desc: You did everything right and your body answered by snapping wide awake the second the room went dark. This is for the woman whose nervous system treats bedtime like a threat — and it will show you, from the body and without force, how to let that old alarm finally stand down.
books.long_desc:
It is the cruelest trick. You are flattened with tiredness all day, you finally lie down, and the instant the lights go out your body floods with a quiet, wordless alarm. Your jaw sets. Your shoulders climb toward your ears. Your heart picks up for no reason you can name, and some watchful part of you scans the dark for a danger that is nowhere in the room. You are bracing for something — and you couldn't tell anyone what.

This book is for the woman who is safe but cannot feel safe. The one who holds it together all day at a demanding job and then lies awake at 2am, certain her body has simply forgotten how to do the one thing it was built for. You are not weak. You are not failing at rest. Your nervous system is doing exactly what it learned to do — staying ready, staying on, treating the stillness of bedtime as the most dangerous moment of the day because that is when it finally has nothing left to manage.

Working gently, from the body and never against it, you will come to recognize the false alarm for what it is. You'll feel the difference between being safe and feeling safe — and learn why your mind already knows the room is fine while your body refuses to believe it. You'll find the braced places you've been carrying for so long you stopped noticing them, and you'll learn to bring warmth back to them, breath by breath, instead of bullying them into silence. You'll come to understand the false alarm not as a malfunction to fix but as a frightened part of you that has been on watch far too long, and is finally allowed to rest.

This is not a protocol. There is no tracker here, no perfect routine, no willpower required. Nothing in these pages will optimize your sleep or hand you a checklist to fail at. This book will not ask you to push through, override, or out-discipline your own body — because that is the very pressure that keeps the alarm ringing.

What it offers instead is slower, and truer: a way to be in your body at night that doesn't feel like a fight. Your body is not your enemy. It has been guarding you for years, faithfully, against a danger that is no longer here. Tonight, you can begin to let it know.

Book 1 of the Felt Sense Publishing sleep anxiety series.

books.title: Everything Arrives the Moment I Lie Down
books.subtitle: For the woman who holds the whole day together until bedtime, when it all floods in at once — a body-first way to make room to rest
books.desc: You kept it together all day, and the second your body went still the whole flood arrived — every task, every worry, every feeling you had no room for. This is for the woman who is drowning at bedtime, and it offers a slower, somatic way to let the day move through you so there is finally space to sleep.
books.long_desc:
You made it through the day. You answered everything, carried everyone, kept the surface calm. And then you got into bed, your body finally went still — and all of it arrived at once. The unsent message. Tomorrow's deadline. The thing you said in the meeting. A wave of feeling with no name and nowhere to go, crashing in the moment there was finally quiet enough to feel it. There is no room left in you to think, let alone sleep. You are not tired. You are flooded.

This book is for the woman whose capacity is full long before her head hits the pillow. The one who is so good at holding it together that the holding only lets go at night, when it's least welcome. You spend your days at the edge of what you can carry, and bedtime is when the dam you've been bracing all day finally gives. It feels like too much because it is too much — and it has been arriving with nowhere to land for longer than you've let yourself admit.

Working from the body and never by force, you will come to recognize the flood for what it is: not a character flaw, not a failure to manage your time, but a nervous system that was given no room to discharge the day while it was happening. You'll learn why stillness feels like a threat when you've spent twelve hours outrunning your own overwhelm. You'll find where the day lives in your body — the held breath, the tight chest, the shoulders that never came down — and you'll learn to let it move through you and out, slowly, instead of damming it up until dark. You'll discover what it feels like to have space inside you again, the kind of room that has to exist before sleep can find you.

This is not a productivity system for your nights. There is no brain-dump checklist to perfect, no morning routine to blame yourself for skipping, no optimization. This book will not ask you to manage the flood more efficiently or push through one more thing. The pushing is what fills you to the brim in the first place.

What it offers instead is permission to stop catching everything. You were never meant to hold an entire day in a single body with no place to set it down. Tonight, you can begin to let some of it go.

books.title: One Thought, Then a Thousand, in the Dark
books.subtitle: For the bright, busy mind that turns a single 2am worry into a sleepless interrogation — and a gentler way down from the spiral
books.desc: One small worry at lights-out becomes a thousand by 3am, each thought pulling you further down. This is for the woman whose sharp mind turns on her the moment she lies down, and it offers a body-first way to stop feeding the spiral and find the ground beneath the noise.
books.long_desc:
It always starts so small. One thought as you settle into the dark — a half-finished task, an offhand comment, a worry the size of a pebble. And then your mind, the same quick mind that serves you all day, takes that single thought and multiplies it. One becomes ten. Ten becomes a hundred. By 3am you are no longer worrying about one thing; you are running an interrogation, building a case against your decisions, your relationships, your entire life, each thought feeding the next in a downward pull you cannot find the bottom of. You are exhausted and wired, certain that if you could just think it all the way through, you could finally rest.

This book is for the woman whose greatest strength becomes her nightly undoing. The analytical, capable mind that solves problems all day and then, in the dark, turns that same sharpness on itself with nothing left to solve. You don't have a thinking problem. You have a nervous system in spin, using thought the way a body in danger uses adrenaline — to stay ready, to stay ahead, to never be caught off guard. The spiral isn't keeping you safe. It only feels like it is.

Working from the body, gently and without force, you will come to recognize the spiral for what it is: not a search for answers but a loop your mind runs when your body doesn't feel safe enough to stop. You'll learn why 3am thinking always pulls downward, and why the answer was never to think your way out. You'll find the ground beneath the noise — the breath, the weight of your body on the bed, the felt sense of here-and-now that the spiral spins you away from. You'll learn to feel the moment the loop begins to grip, and to come back down into your body before it carries you off, not by silencing your mind but by giving it somewhere solid to land.

This is not a thought-stopping technique or a mental-discipline program. There is no method here for arguing with your thoughts, no checklist, no optimization. This book will not ask you to control your mind or win the argument it's having at 3am. The fight is the fuel — the harder you wrestle the spiral, the faster it spins.

What it offers instead is a way out that goes through the body, not the mind. You do not have to think your way to peace tonight. You only have to come back to the ground. It has been beneath you the whole time.

Book 3 of the Felt Sense Publishing sleep anxiety series.

books.title: There's No Room Left in Me
books.subtitle: For the high-functioning woman whose body is past capacity — when too much arrives at once and there's nowhere left to put it
books.desc: Some days the inbox, the texts, the deadlines, and the low roar in your chest all arrive at the same moment, and your body simply runs out of room. This is a book for the woman who keeps performing competence while her nervous system quietly floods. It won't hand you another system to manage — it will help you feel where the flood lives and let some of it move.
books.long_desc:
It usually doesn't announce itself. One more message lands, one more person needs something, and somewhere behind your sternum a door you didn't know was open slams shut. Your chest goes tight. Your thinking goes flat and fast at the same time. You answer the email anyway, voice level, hands steady, while inside there is no room left — no room to think, no room to feel, no room to be a person instead of a function.

This book is for you: the one everyone counts on, the one who holds the plan for the household and the team and the group chat, the one who has gotten very good at looking fine while her body quietly exceeds its capacity. You may have been told you're 'so organized,' 'so on top of it.' What no one sees is the flood underneath — the sense that everything is arriving at once and there is nowhere to put any of it.

You'll come to recognize overwhelm not as a character flaw or a time-management failure, but as a nervous system that has taken on more than it can hold and started bracing. You'll learn what the flood actually is in the body — the rising pressure, the narrowed breath, the way attention scatters when there's too much to track. You'll find small, portable ways to make a little room again: ways that fit on a train, in a stairwell, in the ninety seconds before the next call. Not to clear your plate. To come back into your body while it's still full.

This book will not promise to make the overwhelm stop. It will not optimize your morning or hand you a tracker or tell you to do more, faster, calmer. There is no protocol here that overrides what your body is telling you. What's offered instead is recognition — the relief of understanding what is happening and why it makes such complete sense — and the beginning of a different relationship with your own capacity. One where reaching your limit is information, not failure.

You are not too much. You have simply been holding too much, for too long, with too little room to set any of it down. That's not a flaw in you. That's a body doing exactly what it was built to do, asking, finally, to be felt.

You're allowed to stop filling the room. You're allowed to take up the room you're in.

books.title: The Part of You That Never Stands Down
books.subtitle: For the woman whose mind never goes off duty — scanning every room, every tone, every face for a threat that doesn't come
books.desc: You read the room before you enter it. You catch the shift in someone's voice before they finish the sentence. You can't remember the last time your guard was fully down. This is a book for the woman whose body never stops scanning — and a gentle way to let the watcher, finally, sit down.
books.long_desc:
You notice everything. The slight change in your manager's tone on the call. The text that took an hour too long to come back. The expression that crossed someone's face for half a second before they smoothed it over. You catch it all, instantly, before you've even decided to look — and your body responds before your mind can ask whether there was anything to respond to. Shoulders up. Breath shallow. A quiet engine running underneath the day that never quite turns off.

This book is for you: the one who is always a little bit braced, always reading the room, always half a step ahead of a danger that, most of the time, never arrives. You might call it being perceptive, being prepared, being good at your job. And it is those things. It is also a nervous system that learned, somewhere along the way, that safety meant never being caught off guard — and never got the message that it's allowed to stand down.

You'll come to recognize the watcher not as anxiety to be argued with, but as an old form of protection that is still on duty long after the shift should have ended. You'll learn what hypervigilance feels like from the inside — the scanning, the bracing, the way rest itself can feel unsafe because letting your guard down once cost you something. You'll find small, body-first ways to signal to your system that this room, right now, is not the room it's still watching for. Not to switch off your awareness. To let it soften when it can.

This book will not promise to make you stop noticing, or hand you a technique to override the alarm. There is nothing here that bypasses what your body knows or forces calm onto a system that doesn't yet believe it's safe. What's offered instead is recognition — the deep relief of understanding why you are the way you are, and that it was never paranoia, never too much, never a flaw. It was a body keeping you safe the only way it knew how.

The watcher in you is not your enemy. It has been working a very long shift, alone, with no relief. It doesn't need to be silenced. It needs to be thanked, and slowly, gently, shown that it's finally allowed to rest.

You don't have to be on guard to be okay. You're allowed to put the watch down.

Book 2 of the Felt Sense Publishing somatic healing series.

books.title: Everyone Else Seems to Have It Together
books.subtitle: For the working parent measuring against the calm ones — when every other family looks effortless and you're sure you're the one falling behind
books.desc: At drop-off, in the group chat, on every scrolling feed, everyone else looks calm and capable — and you measure your frantic insides against their smooth outsides and always come up short. This is a body-first companion for the parent caught in the exhausting habit of comparison. It won't help you win the comparison. That's the point.
books.long_desc:
It's drop-off, and you watch the other parent — unhurried, coffee in hand, kid's hair actually brushed — and something in you sinks. They have it figured out. You're the one who forgot the show-and-tell thing, again, who yelled in the car, again, whose insides are a tangle of worry while everyone else seems to glide. You scroll at night and it's worse: the calm homes, the easy marriages, the parents who clearly aren't lying awake like this. You measure yourself against all of them, and you always, always come up short.

This book is for the parent who lives behind that ruler. Who compares their churning interior to everyone else's composed surface and reads the gap as proof of their own failure. Who feels behind — at parenting, at calm, at coping, at life. Who can't enjoy a good moment because someone, somewhere, seems to be doing it better. The comparison feels like information. It isn't. It's a high-precision instrument pointed at the one target it can never measure fairly: yourself, from the inside, against people you only see from the outside. You are not behind. You are comparing your felt experience to a highlight reel, and the math was never going to work.

Here you'll come to recognize comparison's grip in the body — the sink in the gut, the tightening, the particular small clench of measuring and losing. You'll learn that you cannot win this game and you were never meant to play it. You'll feel what it is to come off the ruler and back into your own skin: the breath that's yours, the ground that's yours, the life that is actually, only, yours to live. Not to become the calm parent at drop-off. To stop needing to.

This is not a method for finally measuring up. There's no leveling the playing field, no out-performing the other parents, no optimizing yourself into the lead. Nothing here will help you win the comparison — that's the point. We will not feed the instrument better data. We will gently turn it off. Nothing in these pages promises you'll stop noticing the gap on command; the point is that the gap stops being a verdict.

You are allowed to be exactly where you are, unfavorably compared to no one. You are allowed to set the ruler down. The comparison will rise and the comparison will ease, the way weather passes, and you can learn to let it move through a body that is busy living its own life. You don't have to win. You get to come home to yourself instead.

books.title: The Body Sounds the Alarm Before You Know Why
books.subtitle: For the working parent braced against a danger with no name — when your chest tightens in the school pickup line and nothing is actually wrong
books.desc: Your heart is pounding in the carpool lane and there is no emergency — just the ordinary Tuesday and a body convinced something is about to go wrong. This is a gentle, body-first companion for the parent whose alarm keeps firing when there's no real danger. It will not talk you out of the feeling. It will help your nervous system learn, slowly, that it is safe to stand down.
books.long_desc:
You're third in the pickup line, hands at ten and two, and your whole body is braced. Heart fast. Jaw tight. A bright wire of dread running from your throat to your stomach, ready for something terrible — and there is nothing. The kids are fine. The afternoon is ordinary. But your body didn't get the message, and it's been like this for a while now: the alarm going off in rooms that aren't burning.

This book is for you. For the parent who lies awake cataloguing what could go wrong tomorrow. Who feels the surge of adrenaline at a normal email, a closing door, a child a half-second slow to answer. Who has been told to calm down so many times it has started to feel like a personal failing. It isn't. Your alarm system is not broken — it is loud, and faithful, and firing at shadows because somewhere along the way it learned the world needs constant guarding. You are not overreacting. You are over-protected by a body that loves you clumsily.

Here you'll come to recognize the false alarm for what it is. You'll feel where the bracing actually lives — the held breath, the locked shoulders, the belly that won't soften. You'll learn to meet the surge without arguing with it, to let your feet find the floor, to send your nervous system the slow signal it's been waiting for: not now, not this, you can set it down. You'll begin to tell the difference between a real threat and the echo of one, and that difference is where your afternoons come back to you.

This is not a method for conquering anxiety. There is no white-knuckling here, no mind over matter, no pushing through. We will not override your body or flood it with intensity it can't hold. Nothing in these pages will make the fear disappear on command, and that is not the point. The point is that a braced body can learn, gently, that the danger has passed — and you can be the steady presence that teaches it.

You are allowed to feel this and still be okay. You are allowed to come down slowly. Let the engine tick. Let your shoulders drop a quarter inch. You don't have to be ready for the worst to be a good parent. You just have to come home to your own body, one unhurried breath at a time.

books.title: The Parent You Were Before All This
books.subtitle: For the working parent grieving an unnamed loss — the self, the ease, the life you pictured, that anxiety arrived to fill the space of
books.desc: Beneath the anxiety is a loss you may never have let yourself name — the person you were, the ease you had, the life you imagined would feel different than this. This is a body-first companion for the parent carrying ungrieved sorrow as constant worry. It won't rush you toward acceptance. It will make room for what you've lost, so your body doesn't have to hold it as fear.
books.long_desc:
You found the old photo on your phone — you, before, laughing at something, looking lighter than you can remember feeling in years. And something caught in your throat. Not quite sadness, not quite longing. A grief with no funeral: for the self you used to be, the spontaneity you traded, the marriage or the body or the years that aren't coming back, the version of this life you pictured that simply never arrived. Nobody died. And still, something was lost, and your body has been carrying that unnamed loss the only way it knows how — as a low, constant anxiety that never quite explains itself.

This book is for the parent who is quietly grieving and calling it stress. Who feels a pang they can't place at the school gate, at bedtime, at the sink. Who misses someone, and the someone is themselves. Who has had no time and no permission to mourn the things that ended when this chapter began — because the kids need dinner, because grief feels ungrateful when you love them this much, because there was never a moment to stop. The anxiety is real. But some of it is sorrow wearing the only coat it was given. Loss that can't be felt doesn't disappear. It waits in the body, and it knocks as fear.

Here you'll come to recognize the grief hidden inside the worry — the heaviness, the tightness in the chest that isn't quite dread, the tears that arrive sideways at odd moments. You'll learn that grief is not a problem to solve but a weight to be allowed, slowly, with breath and with kindness. You'll feel what it is to let your body finally hold the loss as loss instead of as alarm — to let it move through you, soften, settle, the way it was always meant to. Not to get over what's gone. To stop bracing against the missing of it.

This is not a method for moving on. There's no closure to achieve, no stages to march through, no pushing past it. We will not tell you to count your blessings over a body that needs to mourn, and we will not force open a grief you aren't ready to meet. Nothing here promises the ache will leave on schedule. The point isn't to be done. The point is to make room — to let what was lost be lost, and felt, and held.

You are allowed to miss who you were and still love who you've become. You are allowed to grieve a life no one died from. The sorrow will rise and the sorrow will ease, the way weather moves through, and you can learn to let it pass through a body soft enough to feel it. You don't have to carry it as fear anymore. You're allowed to simply, finally, be sad — and to let that be tender instead of dangerous.

books.long_desc:
It's 6:10pm. This book is for the parent who lives with that private verdict. Who reads ordinary struggle as personal failure. Who can take in any criticism as confirmation and no reassurance as true. Who is exhausted not only by the doing but by the hiding — the constant low effort of seeming fine. Who flinches at the word should. Shame isn't the truth about you. It's an old, heavy weather system you learned to live under, probably long before you were a parent, and it has been lying to you in your own voice. You are not the defect you've been covering. There is no defect. There is a tender place that got taught it was a flaw.

You'll feel what it is to stop overworking the cover story for a single breath, to let your body be seen by you, gently, without the verdict. Not to fix what's wrong. You are allowed to be a struggling, ordinary, good-enough parent and not a fraud. You are allowed to set the stone down. It's the part of you that loves your children so fiercely it volunteered for a shift with no end. But it's been on duty too long, guarding against dangers that mostly never come, and it has forgotten how to rest. You are not paranoid. You don't have to guard every moment to be a good parent. You're allowed, finally, to come off duty — and rest.

books.desc: The kids are asleep. The doors are locked. And still some part of you is up, listening, scanning, half-waiting for the sound that means you're needed. This is a book for the working parent whose nervous system never quite goes off duty — who can't remember the last time their body believed it was safe to stop watching. It won't tell you to relax or 'just be present.' It will show you why the watcher won't stand down, and how to let it rest its eyes without abandoning your post.
books.long_desc:
Everyone is finally asleep. The house is quiet. The doors are locked, the stove is off, the children are breathing softly down the hall. By every reasonable measure, you are safe. And yet some part of you is still up — still listening, still scanning the dark for the sound that means a small person needs you, still braced for the thing that hasn't happened and probably won't. You can't seem to call it off. You can't remember the last time your body believed the day was actually over.

This book is for you — the working parent whose mind never quite clocks out. The one who tracks every cough through the baby monitor, who reads the room before they've taken off their coat, who lies down exhausted and finds that the watching simply continues without them. You are not paranoid. You are not high-strung. Your nervous system has been keeping you and the people you love alive, and it has decided it cannot afford to look away.

Gently, and without ever asking you to 'just relax,' you'll come to recognize the watcher for what it is: not anxiety to be defeated, but a part of you that took on the job of vigilance and never got told it could rest. You'll feel where that scanning lives in your body — the held breath, the eyes that won't soften, the shoulders that listen. You'll learn what it is to thank that part instead of fighting it, and to let it set its weight down, for a moment, onto ground that can hold you both. You'll begin to notice the difference between a real signal and an old habit of bracing — and to let your body, slowly, relearn what 'off duty' feels like.

This book will not promise to switch the watching off. It won't optimize your hypervigilance into productivity or hand you a routine that turns rest into one more task. It will not ask you to override the part of you that loves so fiercely it cannot look away. There is no fix here, and that is the point. The watcher was never the enemy. It was just never relieved.

The practices are small enough to do without anyone noticing — a softening of the eyes in a meeting, one slow breath while the monitor glows, a hand on your own chest in the dark. They meet you on duty, where you actually live.

You'll walk away knowing what your body is doing and why it has every reason to keep watch. Not cured. Not finally at ease. But able, now and then, to feel the ground take the watching — and to let the part of you that never clocks out close its eyes, just for a breath, knowing you are still here.

You are allowed to rest your eyes. The love does not require you to never look away.

Book 2 of the Felt Sense Publishing somatic healing series.
